title: Chicken tikka masala
subtitle: Charred yoghurt-marinated chicken in a tomato-cream sauce.
excerpt: Yoghurt-and-spice-marinated chicken, charred fast under a hot grill, then folded into a tomato-cream sauce built on onion, ginger, garlic, garam masala. Ninety minutes start to finish, an hour of which is the marinade. Mild enough for children, rich enough for company.
sourceNotes:
Colonel A. R. Kenney-Herbert ('Wyvern'), Culinary Jottings for Madras (1878): public-domain source for the Anglo-Indian curry tradition that fed into British-Indian restaurant cookery a century later. Wyvern's recipes for spiced poultry braises share the conceptual base of marinated meat plus a separately built spiced sauce.

The dish itself is later: chicken tikka masala as it now exists is a post-1960s British-Indian restaurant invention, often attributed to Shish Mahal in Glasgow in the late 1960s, though Bangladeshi-British restaurateurs across London and Birmingham developed parallel versions. The precise origin is contested in the food-writing trade press and not settled in any single public-domain source.

Proportions and method here draw on the published technique of the long-running London-Indian restaurant tradition rather than a single citable text.

recipe.freezeNotes: The finished masala sauce freezes well for three months. Cool quickly, freeze in portions, defrost overnight in the fridge before reheating. Re-cooked chicken can get rubbery on freezing; better to freeze the sauce alone and grill fresh chicken when you reheat.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly in a wide sauté pan. Triple-batch the sauce alone for the freezer and grill chicken to order each time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken can marinate for up to twenty-four hours in the fridge; longer is better than shorter. The sauce can be made a day ahead and reheated; the flavour improves overnight.
recipe.temperatureNote: the grill at full power; some ovens call it broiler

recipeTools.notes: Or a roasting pan with a wire rack. The chicken cooks under the overhead grill at full heat.
Wide 28 cm pan for the sauce.
For grating ginger and garlic; a paste base for the sauce.
For turning the chicken under the grill.
For blitzing the sauce to a smooth finish; a stick blender works.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bite-size pieces of meat (usually chicken or lamb) marinated in spiced yoghurt and cooked at high heat — traditionally in a clay tandoor oven, at home most often under an overhead grill or in a hot oven. The yoghurt tenderises the meat and carries the spice into the surface.

Cooking ground or whole spices briefly in hot fat (oil or ghee) before liquid is added, to release their fat-soluble aromatic compounds. The spices smell sharply different once bloomed — toastier, deeper, less raw. Skipping the bloom leaves the finished sauce tasting muddy and one-note.

content.text: Chicken tikka masala is the British-Indian restaurant classic that became, by widely-quoted polling, the national dish of Britain. Yoghurt-and-spice-marinated chicken pieces are charred fast under a hot grill, then folded into a tomato-and-cream sauce built on onion, ginger, garlic, garam masala. Ninety minutes total, an hour of which is the marinade. Mild enough for children, rich enough for company.
The recipe is two halves cooked in parallel. The
tikka

marinade does its work in the fridge: yoghurt tenderises the chicken while turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and chilli powder season every face of every piece. The sauce builds on the hob: a wide pan, onion sweated soft, ginger and garlic paste in next, then tomato, then cream. The two halves meet only at the very end, when the charred chicken is folded into the warm sauce.

Three working notes set the dish apart from a generic curry. First, the marinade wants a full hour at minimum; the protein needs that long for the yoghurt to start softening the fibres. Second, the chicken wants real char under a hot grill: black-edged spots that bring smokiness into the sauce, well beyond surface colour. Third, the masala sauce is finished smooth, blitzed or sieved at the end so the texture is velvet, never chunky.

attrs.title: Why the grill at full power
attrs.body: A domestic grill at full power gets to around 280°C at the rack, which is close enough to a tandoor's effect on the surface of the chicken. The yoghurt-coated pieces blister and char in eight or nine minutes a side. A medium heat won't get you there: the chicken cooks through but stays pale, and the dish loses the smoky edge. Set the chicken close to the grill element (one rack down from the top) and don't be afraid of the black spots.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless; cut each into 4 large chunks
a small handful, chopped, to finish

content.text: Two pans go in parallel: a grill tray for the chicken and a wide sauté pan for the sauce. A microplane is the fastest way to grate fresh ginger and garlic into a paste; a small mortar and pestle works too. A stick blender or high-powered jug blender finishes the sauce smooth at the end.
Method
Marinate the chicken

Whisk together {{greek-yoghurt}} of Greek yoghurt, {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{garlic}} crushed garlic cloves, the juice of {{lemon}}, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt in a large bowl. Add the chicken pieces and turn to coat every face. Cover and refrigerate for at least an hour; up to twenty-four hours is better.
Build the sauce base

Warm {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil and {{unsalted-butter}} of butter together in a wide sauté pan over a medium heat. Tip in {{onion}} finely chopped onions with a pinch of salt. Cook for fifteen minutes, stirring every couple of minutes, until the onions are deeply golden and starting to turn jammy at the edges. Patience here: the dish leans heavily on the colour and sweetness of the onions.
Bloom the spices

Stir in {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger and {{garlic}} grated garlic cloves; cook for a minute until fragrant. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, and {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin. Cook for two minutes, stirring constantly, until the oil starts to separate from the paste and the smell turns toasty rather than sharp. This is
blooming the spices
; it's the step that takes the dish from muddy to deep.
Simmer the sauce

Tip in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them with the back of the spoon. Half-fill the empty tin with water and add that too. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook for twenty minutes, stirring occasionally. The sauce should darken from bright red to deep brick-red and start to look glossy.
Grill the chicken

While the sauce simmers, heat the grill to its highest setting. Line a grill tray with foil and set a wire rack on top. Lift the marinated chicken pieces out of the bowl, letting the excess marinade drip back in, and arrange the pieces in a single layer on the rack with a little space between each. Slide the tray under the grill, one rack down from the top element, and cook for eight minutes. Turn each piece with tongs and cook for another six to eight minutes until charred in spots and cooked through. Don't be afraid of the black edges; they're where the flavour is.
Finish the sauce smooth

Pull the sauce pan off the heat. Blitz the sauce with a stick blender until smooth, or tip into a jug blender and back. Return the smooth sauce to the pan over a low heat. Crumble in {{fenugreek-leaves}} of dried fenugreek leaves between your fingers — the fragrance lift here is unmistakable and the dish lacks something without it. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream. Taste and adjust with fine salt.
Fold in the chicken

Slide the grilled chicken pieces off the rack into the sauce, scraping up any juices and char that pooled under the rack. Turn the chicken in the sauce to coat. Simmer together for two or three minutes, no more, just to bring the chicken back up to temperature and let the flavours marry. Scatter with chopped fresh coriander. Serve with basmati rice, naan, or both.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Tikka masala goes wrong at the same places most curries do: bland sauce, dry chicken, gritty texture from raw spices.

items.cause: Spices not bloomed, or onions not browned enough.
items.fix: The two-minute bloom of spices in hot oil is the difference between a deep sauce and a muddy one. The onions need fifteen minutes of patient cooking before the rest goes in; if you rushed them at ten minutes, the dish runs sweet rather than savoury. Cook a little longer next time.

items.symptom: Chicken pieces dry and stringy after grilling.
items.cause: Used breast instead of thigh, or grilled too long.
items.fix: Boneless thigh stays juicy under the grill; breast turns to leather in the same time. If you must use breast, drop the grill time to five minutes a side and finish the cook in the sauce. Eight to nine minutes a side is the right window for thigh.

items.symptom: Sauce harsh, with a raw-spice edge.
items.cause: Spices not cooked long enough before the tomato went in.
items.fix: Bloom the spices in the hot fat until you see the oil separating around the edges of the paste. That's the visual cue. Throw the tomato in too early and the spices stay raw and gritty in the finished sauce.

items.symptom: Sauce split, with a layer of oil on top.
items.cause: Cream added at too high a heat, or cooked too long after.
items.fix: Pull the sauce off the heat before adding the cream, then return to a low heat. Don't let it boil after the cream is in; a gentle warm-through is all it needs. If the sauce has split, whisk in a tablespoon of cold cream off the heat to bring it back.

items.cause: Tinned tomatoes were acidic; not enough sugar from the onions.
items.fix: Some tins of tomatoes are sharper than others. A pinch of sugar at the cream stage rounds the acidity off. Cook the onions longer next time; deep gold onions add sweetness that balances the tomato.

content.text: Variations

Paneer tikka masala swaps cubes of paneer for the chicken; the marinade and method are the same, though the paneer cooks in three minutes a side under the grill rather than eight. Prawn tikka masala uses raw king prawns; cook for two minutes a side under the grill. For a richer dairy-free version, replace the cream with full-fat coconut milk; the sauce takes on a coconut sweetness and pairs well with rice.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The marinade can be made up to twenty-four hours ahead; the chicken benefits from the longer soak. The sauce alone holds for two days in the fridge and freezes for three months. Re-cooking grilled chicken can leave it rubbery; better to freeze the sauce alone, defrost it overnight in the fridge, and grill fresh chicken when you reheat. Cold leftovers reheat well in a covered pan over a low heat with a splash of water.
Where this dish lives

Chicken tikka masala is not an Indian dish. It is a British-Indian dish: a creation of the post-war Bangladeshi-British and Punjabi-British restaurant trade, most often credited to Shish Mahal in Glasgow's West End in the late 1960s, though similar versions emerged in parallel across London and Birmingham. The story (probably embellished, definitely repeated) is that a customer asked for some gravy with his chicken tikka, and a Glaswegian-Bangladeshi cook poured over a tin of tomato soup mixed with cream and spices. The dish stuck.

What is true is that the dish is firmly British in lineage; you won't find it on menus in Delhi or Mumbai except as a tourist concession. The Anglo-Indian curry tradition Colonel Kenney-Herbert wrote about in 1878, adapting Madras court cookery to British tastes, is the older ancestor; tikka masala is the late-twentieth-century descendant. Robin Cook, then British Foreign Secretary, called it the national dish of Britain in a 2001 speech; the phrase stuck, partly as a joke and partly because it was true.

Served at home with basmati rice and a stack of naan, it is one of the warmest mid-week dinners a small kitchen can produce. It is also the dish that most teaches a home cook the underlying grammar of an Indian-tradition curry: marinated meat, soffritto base, bloomed spices, tomato simmer, cream finish. Even when the dish itself is not Indian, the grammar is. Once you cook this, dal makhani and butter chicken and rogan josh all start to make sense in the same way.
